title: Lichen Planus
oneLiner: Chronic, autoimmune, T-cell-mediated mucocutaneous disease with violaceous, polygonal, pruritic papules; oral form is premalignant.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
- **Classic vignette:** A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of intensely itchy, flat-topped, purple bumps appearing on her wrists, ankles, and lower back. She also notices a lacy, white pattern inside her cheeks that doesn’t scrape off. There’s no prior skin disease, no new medications, and she is generally well. On examination you see violaceous, polygonal papules with fine white lines (Wickham striae) on the surface. The oral mucosa reveals bilateral, reticular, white streaks—asymptomatic but persistent.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Violaceous, polygonal papules:* Classic primary lesion of lichen planus (LP). The colour reflects underlying inflammation and altered dermal blood flow; the polygon shape results from confluent papules.
  - *Intense pruritus:* Hallmark of cutaneous LP; due to T-cell mediated release of pruritogenic cytokines.
  - *Distribution: wrists, ankles, lower back, flexor surfaces:* These are “lichenoid” sites—areas subject to friction. The Koebner phenomenon (isomorphic response) occurs, where trauma triggers new lesions.
  - *Lacy white striae (Wickham striae):* Pathognomonic; represent focal hypergranulosis in the epidermis.
  - *Oral lesions: bilateral, reticular, white streaks:* Asymptomatic in most; key clue to differentiate from other dermatoses.
  - *No new medications, no systemic illness:* Points to idiopathic LP (most common); however, you must still rule out drug-induced and HCV-associated forms.

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - *Hypertrophic lichen planus:* Thick, hyperkeratotic, violaceous plaques on the shins, often with a psoriasiform appearance. Can be mistaken for psoriasis or stasis dermatitis.
  - *Bullous lichen planus:* Tense or flaccid blisters arising on pre-existing LP lesions. Simulates pemphigoid or dermatitis herpetiformis.
  - *Linear lichen planus:* Lesions follow the lines of Blaschko (developmental lines). Mimics linear psoriasis or lichen striatus.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
- **Plain-language definition:** Lichen planus is an inflammatory skin and mucous membrane disorder where your own immune cells (T lymphocytes) attack the deepest layer of the epidermis (basal layer), causing itchy purple bumps, lacy white patches in the mouth, and sometimes nail or scalp changes. It is not contagious.

- **Why this matters clinically:**
  - **Frequency:** Common in adults 30–60 years; accounts for ~1% of dermatology visits. Oral LP is the most common non-infectious oral mucosal disease.
  - **Stakes:** The oral form (especially the erosive subtype) carries a 1–2% risk of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) over 10–20 years. Hypertrophic LP can be highly resistant to therapy. Nail and scalp involvement may lead to irreversible scarring (pterygium, scarring alopecia).
  - **Common pitfalls:** 
    - Relying solely on clinical exam without checking oral mucosa → missing the oral component.
    - Forgetting to test for hepatitis C (HCV), which is associated in up to 20–30% of cases (especially in Southern Europe, Asia).
    - Misdiagnosing oral LP as leukoplakia or candidiasis (white lesions that scrape off in candidiasis; leukoplakia is usually unilateral and not lacy).
    - Not recognising that drug-induced LP can mimic idiopathic LP exactly—always obtain a full medication history (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antimalarials, gold).
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
- **The story:** Lichen planus is an autoimmune disease driven by CD8+ cytotoxic T cells that recognize an antigen (likely a self-peptide or viral/hapten-modified self-protein) presented on MHC class I at the basal layer of the epidermis or oral epithelium. These T cells induce apoptosis of basal keratinocytes, causing a characteristic band-like (lichenoid) inflammatory infiltrate hugging the dermo‑epidermal junction.

- **Mechanism → Sign:**
  - *Apoptosis of basal keratinocytes →* The damaged cells shrink, become eosinophilic, and are extruded as **Civatte bodies** (colloid bodies) – seen histologically as dyskeratotic cells.
  - *The immune attack triggers keratinocyte hyperproliferation →* **Acanthosis** (thickened epidermis), **hypergranulosis** (especially prominent, producing the white lines of Wickham striae).
  - *Basal cell destruction + influx of inflammatory cells →* **Saw-tooth rete ridges** (irregular downward projections of the epidermis) and a **band-like lymphocytic infiltrate** at the interface.
  - *Cytokine release (e.g., IL‑31, TNF‑α) →* **Intense pruritus**, the most common patient complaint in cutaneous LP.
  - *In oral LP, the same mechanism occurs but the environment is moist and susceptible to trauma →* The reticular pattern reflects hypergranulosis, while the erosive form results from severe basal cell loss, leaving denuded mucosa.

- **Connect to pattern recognition cues:**
  - The **Koebner phenomenon** (lesions at sites of trauma) occurs because any injury to the epidermis exposes basal layer antigens, provoking the T-cell attack.
  - **Nail LP**: The nail matrix is a site of active epithelial turnover; T-cell attack causes thinning, splitting, and eventually **pterygium** (scarring of the nail bed).
  - **Scalp LP** (lichen planopilaris): Attack on hair follicle epithelium leads to **perifollicular erythema**, **scaling**, and irreversible **scarring alopecia**.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Specific Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common, ~70%) | Unknown – likely autoimmune | Middle-aged adult, no other clues |
| **Infectious** | Hepatitis C virus (HCV) | Patient from endemic area (Egypt, Italy), abnormal LFTs, or known HCV |
| **Drug-induced** | NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine), gold, interferon-α, beta-blockers | Onset within weeks to months of starting drug; oral involvement more common; often resolves when drug stopped |
| **Dental materials** | Amalgam fillings (mercury) | Oral LP directly touching a metal filling; rarely, contact allergy to mercury |
| **Autoimmune** | Associated with other autoimmune diseases (alopecia areata, vitiligo, primary biliary cholangitis) | Patient has one of these; family history of autoimmunity |
| **Psychological stress** | Exacerbation, not cause | Onset or flares during periods of high stress |

- **Most common cause: Idiopathic.** Always consider drug-induced and HCV first, because they are reversible if addressed.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis is standard. The **Wickham striae** are pathognomonic for cutaneous LP when present. No formal scoring system is needed for diagnosis, but the **Oral Diseases Severity Score (ODSS)** can quantify oral involvement in trials.

- **Tests:**
  - **Skin or oral biopsy (punch of a well-developed papule):**
    - *What:* Histology shows saw-tooth rete ridges, hypergranulosis, band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermo‑epidermal junction, and Civatte bodies.
    - *Why:* Confirm diagnosis when atypical (hypertrophic, bullous, linear) or when oral lesion needs exclusion of dysplasia.
    - *Interpret:* If features are present → LP confirmed. Absence of these features suggests another lichenoid process (e.g., lupus erythematosus, lichenoid drug reaction).
  - **Direct immunofluorescence (DIF):**
    - *What:* Granular deposits of IgM and C3 at the basement membrane zone (colloid bodies may also stain).
    - *Why:* Not routinely needed; helps differentiate from lupus erythematosus (which has granular IgG deposits).
  - **Hepatitis C serology (anti‑HCV, HCV RNA):**
    - *What:* Detect HCV infection.
    - *Why:* Mandatory in all patients with LP, especially if risk factors or if disease is widespread.
    - *Interpret:* Positive → consider treating HCV (can improve LP). Negative → idiopathic.
  - **No routine blood work for inflammation or autoantibodies** (unless suspecting lupus drug reaction).

- **Pattern-recognition:**
  - **Single finding that confirms:** Wickham striae on clinical exam + typical histology (if biopsied).
  - **Single finding that rules out:** Absence of basal vacuolation and band-like infiltrate on biopsy argues strongly against LP (suggest lichen nitidus, psoriasis, or fixed drug eruption).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
- **Immediate (first hour):** No emergency. Reassure the patient that LP is not contagious. If severe itching, prescribe a sedating antihistamine (e.g., **hydroxyzine 25 mg PO TDS**) for sleep. No topical steroid yet.

- **Ward (24-72h):** Typically outpatient, but if widespread (>50% BSA), erosive oral (unable to eat), or hypertrophic, arrange follow-up.
  - **Mild cutaneous (localised):** Potent topical corticosteroid – **clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream** applied to lesions BD for 2–3 weeks. Reassess.
  - **Oral LP – symptomatic (pain, erosions):** Topical corticosteroids (e.g., **dexamethasone 0.1 mg/mL mouthwash** 5 mL swish and spit QID for 2 weeks) or **clobetasol gel** on lesions. For erosive refractory, consider **intralesional triamcinolone acetonide 10 mg/mL** into the perilesional mucosa (max 0.1 mL per site).
  - **Widespread cutaneous (or failed topical):** Oral **prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 4–6 weeks. Use as bridge to steroid-sparing agent.

- **Long-term:**
  - **Chronic cutaneous (recurrent flares):** Phototherapy – narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) 3×/week for 8–12 weeks. PUVA (psoralen + UVA) if UVB fails.
  - **Refractory/ hypertrophic/ nail/scalp:** Second-line agents:
    - **Methotrexate** 7.5–15 mg/week PO or SC (with folic acid 5 mg once weekly, no on methotrexate day).
    - **Acitretin** 10–25 mg/day PO (monitor LFTs, lipids; avoid in pregnancy).
    - **Mycophenolate mofetil** 1–2 g/day PO (for severe oral LP).
  - **Oral LP – long-term risk:** Regular monitoring (every 6–12 months) for malignant transformation. Avoid irritants (spicy food, alcohol, tobacco). Refer to oral medicine if dysplasia found.
  - **HCV-related LP:** Treat the hepatitis C (DAA therapy) – LP may improve or resolve.

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - Do not use systemic corticosteroids long-term without a taper; risk of steroid dependence.
  - Do not prescribe oral terbinafine or griseofulvin (ineffective).
  - Do not assume oral LP is benign: if erosive or plaque-like, biopsy to exclude SCC.
  - Do not give **isotretinoin** for LP (no role; can worsen dryness).
  - Do not use topical tacrolimus continuously on oral mucosa for >2 years (theoretical risk of lymphoma with prolonged use; reserve for refractory cases).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Lichen Planus | Psoriasis (Plaque) | Eczema (Chronic) | Oral Leukoplakia |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Lesion color** | Violaceous, purple | Salmon-pink with silver scale | Ill-defined red, weepy | Whitish or gray, well-defined |
| **Surface** | Flat-topped, polygonal; Wickham striae (fine white lines) | Thick silvery scale (micaceous) | Excoriations, lichenification from scratching | Smooth, plaque-like; no striae |
| **Distribution** | Wrists, ankles, lower back, flexors; oral mucosa (bilateral, reticular) | Extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), scalp, umbilicus | Flexural (antecubital, popliteal); face in children | Usually unilateral, on buccal mucosa, gingiva, tongue |
| **Pruritus** | Severe (cutaneous); oral often asymptomatic | Mild to severe; can be itchy | Very severe (often worse at night) | Usually none |
| **Koebner phenomenon** | Yes (lesions at trauma sites) | Yes | Sometimes | No |
| **Response to scaping** | No scale to scrape; striae unaffected | Auspitz sign (bleeding points) when scale removed | No | No bleeding |
| **Oral involvement** | Very common (reticular, atrophic, erosive) | Rare (geographic tongue if associated) | Not typical | Only oral lesion, no skin disease |
| **Biopsy hallmark** | Band-like lymphocytic infiltrate, saw-tooth rete ridges, Civatte bodies | Parakeratosis, absent granular layer, elongated rete ridges, Munro microabscesses | Spongiosis, vesiculation, perivascular lymphocytosis | Hyperkeratosis, no interface inflammation; may show dysplasia |
| **Key differentiator** | Wickham striae + oral involvement + bilateral | Silver scale + Auspitz sign + extensor distribution | Flexural distribution + history of atopy | Unilateral, whitish plaque without skin lesions; must biopsy to rule out dysplasia |

- **Other mimics:** Lichenoid drug eruption (history of culprit drug; often more widespread, including trunk). Lupus erythematosus (photosensitivity, malar rash, positive ANA, granular IgG on DIF). Lichen sclerosus (white atrophic plaques, no Wickham striae; predilection for anogenital area).
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation** (common) | Dark macules at sites of resolved LP, especially in darker skin | Violaceous-to-brown patches, often persistent for months | Reassure; avoid trauma; use sunscreen; consider topical hydroquinone 2-4% for disfigurement |
| **Scarring alopecia (lichen planopilaris)** | Patchy hair loss on scalp, burning/itching, perifollicular erythema | Perifollicular erythema and scale; tufting; positive pull test | Early potent topical steroids (clobetasol lotion) or intralesional triamcinolone; refer to dermatology |
| **Nail scarring (pterygium)** | Nail thinning, longitudinal ridging, permanent split formation | Dorsal pterygium (scar tissue from nail fold over nail plate) | Potent topical steroids under occlusion; prevention best; deformity is permanent |
| **Oral squamous cell carcinoma** (erosive or plaque-type oral LP, 1–2% risk) | Persistent ulcer, pain, induration, cervical lymphadenopathy | Irregular, friable, non-healing ulcer at site of known oral LP; biopsy shows SCC | Urgent biopsy; refer to oral surgeon / ENT; CT for staging; surgery ± radiation |
| **Hypertrophic LP** | Thickened, verrucous plaques (shins, calves) that are highly pruritic | Painful fissures, risk of secondary infection | Potent topical steroids under occlusion; consider intralesional steroids; may need excision if refractory |

- **Late-presentation patterns (exam stems):** 
  - “A 60-year-old man with known oral LP presents now with a 2-month history of a non-healing tongue ulcer.” → Suspect SCC.
  - “A patient with lichen planus on the shins for years develops a thick, crusted, verrucous plaque.” → Hypertrophic LP vs. SCC.
  - “A woman with scalp itching and patchy hair loss 2 years after initial LP diagnosis.” → Scarring alopecia.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
- **Mnemonic: PRURITIC** (for features of LP)
  - **P** – Purple, Polygonal, Pruritic
  - **R** – Reticular (oral)
  - **U** – Underlying immune (T-cell)
  - **R** – Really itchy
  - **I** – Inflammatory (band-like)
  - **T** – Thickened granular layer (Wickham striae)
  - **I** – Interface dermatitis
  - **C** – Civatte bodies

- **Mnemonic: WICKHAM STRIES** (to remember associations)
  - **W** – Wrists, ankles
  - **I** – Intensely pruritic
  - **C** – Cutaneous + oral
  - **K** – Koebner phenomenon
  - **H** – HCV association
  - **A** – Autoimmune trigger
  - **M** – Medication-induced
  - **S** – Scarring (nail, scalp)
  - **T** – Treatment: topical steroids
  - **R** – Recurrent flares
  - **I** – Itch – treat aggressively
  - **E** – Erosive oral = risk
  - **S** – Squamous cell carcinoma monitoring

- **Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition):**
  1. **Always look inside the mouth** – even if the patient presents only with skin lesions. The oral exam is the quickest way to differentiate LP from other papulosquamous diseases.
  2. **Wickham striae are pathognomonic** – if you see them, you can diagnose LP without a biopsy (in typical cases).
  3. **Koebner phenomenon** – ask about trauma: new lesions after scratching, surgery, or tight clothing. This is much more common in LP than in psoriasis or eczema.
  4. **HCV testing is mandatory** – up to 1 in 4 patients with LP may have HCV, especially in certain populations. Treating HCV can resolve LP.
  5. **Nail LP can mimic fungal infection** – but LP causes multiple nails, usually with bilateral involvement, and no hyphae on culture. Look for dorsal pterygium (virtually pathognomonic for LP).
  6. **Oral LP >10 years duration** – risk of SCC increases over time. Biopsy any erosive or exophytic lesion.
  7. **Hypertrophic LP on shins is often resistant** – may require intralesional corticosteroids or even surgical excision.

- **Common mistakes to avoid:**
  - Prescribing oral antifungals for oral LP (white streaks are not thrush – thrush scrapes off).
  - Using topical corticosteroids in oral LP without monitoring for candidiasis (add antifungal mouthwash if long-term).
  - Ignoring a drug history (e.g., patient on hydrochlorothiazide or ACE inhibitor) – switch to alternative first.
  - Assuming oral reticular LP is always benign – even reticular forms can transform; periodic review is needed.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call a senior (dermatology registrar/consultant):**
  - Suspected malignant transformation (non-healing ulcer, induration, cervical lymph node palpable).
  - Widespread erosive oral LP causing dehydration / inability to eat (consider hospital admission for IV fluids and topical steroid under supervision).
  - Severe hypertrophic LP failing topical therapy (consider intralesional steroids or surgical opinion).
  - Bullous LP with large areas of denuded skin (risk of secondary infection, need diagnosis confirmation).
  - Lichen planopilaris with rapid hair loss (need prompt aggressive therapy to prevent permanent scarring).

- **When to escalate to ICU / crash team:**
  - LP itself does not directly cause acute collapse. However:
    - Anaphylaxis to a drug (if suspected drug-induced LP and the drug is another category – rare).
    - Severe oral LP leading to airway compromise (extremely rare; if laryngeal involvement with stridor – call ENT/anaesthesia).
  - **New NEWS2 thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5; lactate >2 mmol/L from dehydration due to oral lesions; GCS <15 (if sepsis from secondary infected skin lesions).

- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5 or rising:** consider admission for supportive care (e.g., IV fluids, analgesia).
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L:** think sepsis (especially if hypertrophic LP with fissures or erosive oral lesions).
  - **GCS <15 (or any drop from baseline):** exclude hypoglycemia (from oral steroids if on high dose) or sepsis.
  - **Temperature >38.5°C:** source (skin infection, oral candidiasis superinfection) – seek senior advice.
  - **Inability to swallow own saliva** (oral LP) → urgent review; may need nasogastric tube or systemic steroids.

- **Summary escalation guide (quick reference):**
  - *Call senior:* malignant change, inability to eat, rapid hair loss, bullous disease.
  - *Call crash team:* anaphylaxis, airway compromise, severe sepsis unresponsive to initial resuscitation.

rawMarkdown:
## Lichen Planus
**One-liner:** Chronic, autoimmune, T-cell-mediated mucocutaneous disease with violaceous, polygonal, pruritic papules; oral form is premalignant.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

- **Classic vignette:** A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of intensely itchy, flat-topped, purple bumps appearing on her wrists, ankles, and lower back. She also notices a lacy, white pattern inside her cheeks that doesn’t scrape off. There’s no prior skin disease, no new medications, and she is generally well. On examination you see violaceous, polygonal papules with fine white lines (Wickham striae) on the surface. The oral mucosa reveals bilateral, reticular, white streaks—asymptomatic but persistent.

- **Dissect the vignette:**
  - *Violaceous, polygonal papules:* Classic primary lesion of lichen planus (LP). The colour reflects underlying inflammation and altered dermal blood flow; the polygon shape results from confluent papules.
  - *Intense pruritus:* Hallmark of cutaneous LP; due to T-cell mediated release of pruritogenic cytokines.
  - *Distribution: wrists, ankles, lower back, flexor surfaces:* These are “lichenoid” sites—areas subject to friction. The Koebner phenomenon (isomorphic response) occurs, where trauma triggers new lesions.
  - *Lacy white striae (Wickham striae):* Pathognomonic; represent focal hypergranulosis in the epidermis.
  - *Oral lesions: bilateral, reticular, white streaks:* Asymptomatic in most; key clue to differentiate from other dermatoses.
  - *No new medications, no systemic illness:* Points to idiopathic LP (most common); however, you must still rule out drug-induced and HCV-associated forms.

- **Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
  - *Hypertrophic lichen planus:* Thick, hyperkeratotic, violaceous plaques on the shins, often with a psoriasiform appearance. Can be mistaken for psoriasis or stasis dermatitis.
  - *Bullous lichen planus:* Tense or flaccid blisters arising on pre-existing LP lesions. Simulates pemphigoid or dermatitis herpetiformis.
  - *Linear lichen planus:* Lesions follow the lines of Blaschko (developmental lines). Mimics linear psoriasis or lichen striatus.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

- **Plain-language definition:** Lichen planus is an inflammatory skin and mucous membrane disorder where your own immune cells (T lymphocytes) attack the deepest layer of the epidermis (basal layer), causing itchy purple bumps, lacy white patches in the mouth, and sometimes nail or scalp changes. It is not contagious.

- **Why this matters clinically:**
  - **Frequency:** Common in adults 30–60 years; accounts for ~1% of dermatology visits. Oral LP is the most common non-infectious oral mucosal disease.
  - **Stakes:** The oral form (especially the erosive subtype) carries a 1–2% risk of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) over 10–20 years. Hypertrophic LP can be highly resistant to therapy. Nail and scalp involvement may lead to irreversible scarring (pterygium, scarring alopecia).
  - **Common pitfalls:** 
    - Relying solely on clinical exam without checking oral mucosa → missing the oral component.
    - Forgetting to test for hepatitis C (HCV), which is associated in up to 20–30% of cases (especially in Southern Europe, Asia).
    - Misdiagnosing oral LP as leukoplakia or candidiasis (white lesions that scrape off in candidiasis; leukoplakia is usually unilateral and not lacy).
    - Not recognising that drug-induced LP can mimic idiopathic LP exactly—always obtain a full medication history (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antimalarials, gold).

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

- **The story:** Lichen planus is an autoimmune disease driven by CD8+ cytotoxic T cells that recognize an antigen (likely a self-peptide or viral/hapten-modified self-protein) presented on MHC class I at the basal layer of the epidermis or oral epithelium. These T cells induce apoptosis of basal keratinocytes, causing a characteristic band-like (lichenoid) inflammatory infiltrate hugging the dermo‑epidermal junction.

- **Mechanism → Sign:**
  - *Apoptosis of basal keratinocytes →* The damaged cells shrink, become eosinophilic, and are extruded as **Civatte bodies** (colloid bodies) – seen histologically as dyskeratotic cells.
  - *The immune attack triggers keratinocyte hyperproliferation →* **Acanthosis** (thickened epidermis), **hypergranulosis** (especially prominent, producing the white lines of Wickham striae).
  - *Basal cell destruction + influx of inflammatory cells →* **Saw-tooth rete ridges** (irregular downward projections of the epidermis) and a **band-like lymphocytic infiltrate** at the interface.
  - *Cytokine release (e.g., IL‑31, TNF‑α) →* **Intense pruritus**, the most common patient complaint in cutaneous LP.
  - *In oral LP, the same mechanism occurs but the environment is moist and susceptible to trauma →* The reticular pattern reflects hypergranulosis, while the erosive form results from severe basal cell loss, leaving denuded mucosa.

- **Connect to pattern recognition cues:**
  - The **Koebner phenomenon** (lesions at sites of trauma) occurs because any injury to the epidermis exposes basal layer antigens, provoking the T-cell attack.
  - **Nail LP**: The nail matrix is a site of active epithelial turnover; T-cell attack causes thinning, splitting, and eventually **pterygium** (scarring of the nail bed).
  - **Scalp LP** (lichen planopilaris): Attack on hair follicle epithelium leads to **perifollicular erythema**, **scaling**, and irreversible **scarring alopecia**.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Specific Trigger | Stem Clue |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic** (most common, ~70%) | Unknown – likely autoimmune | Middle-aged adult, no other clues |
| **Infectious** | Hepatitis C virus (HCV) | Patient from endemic area (Egypt, Italy), abnormal LFTs, or known HCV |
| **Drug-induced** | NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, antimalarials (hydroxychloroquine), gold, interferon-α, beta-blockers | Onset within weeks to months of starting drug; oral involvement more common; often resolves when drug stopped |
| **Dental materials** | Amalgam fillings (mercury) | Oral LP directly touching a metal filling; rarely, contact allergy to mercury |
| **Autoimmune** | Associated with other autoimmune diseases (alopecia areata, vitiligo, primary biliary cholangitis) | Patient has one of these; family history of autoimmunity |
| **Psychological stress** | Exacerbation, not cause | Onset or flares during periods of high stress |

- **Most common cause: Idiopathic.** Always consider drug-induced and HCV first, because they are reversible if addressed.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis is standard. The **Wickham striae** are pathognomonic for cutaneous LP when present. No formal scoring system is needed for diagnosis, but the **Oral Diseases Severity Score (ODSS)** can quantify oral involvement in trials.

- **Tests:**
  - **Skin or oral biopsy (punch of a well-developed papule):**
    - *What:* Histology shows saw-tooth rete ridges, hypergranulosis, band-like lymphocytic infiltrate at the dermo‑epidermal junction, and Civatte bodies.
    - *Why:* Confirm diagnosis when atypical (hypertrophic, bullous, linear) or when oral lesion needs exclusion of dysplasia.
    - *Interpret:* If features are present → LP confirmed. Absence of these features suggests another lichenoid process (e.g., lupus erythematosus, lichenoid drug reaction).
  - **Direct immunofluorescence (DIF):**
    - *What:* Granular deposits of IgM and C3 at the basement membrane zone (colloid bodies may also stain).
    - *Why:* Not routinely needed; helps differentiate from lupus erythematosus (which has granular IgG deposits).
  - **Hepatitis C serology (anti‑HCV, HCV RNA):**
    - *What:* Detect HCV infection.
    - *Why:* Mandatory in all patients with LP, especially if risk factors or if disease is widespread.
    - *Interpret:* Positive → consider treating HCV (can improve LP). Negative → idiopathic.
  - **No routine blood work for inflammation or autoantibodies** (unless suspecting lupus drug reaction).

- **Pattern-recognition:**
  - **Single finding that confirms:** Wickham striae on clinical exam + typical histology (if biopsied).
  - **Single finding that rules out:** Absence of basal vacuolation and band-like infiltrate on biopsy argues strongly against LP (suggest lichen nitidus, psoriasis, or fixed drug eruption).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

- **Immediate (first hour):** No emergency. Reassure the patient that LP is not contagious. If severe itching, prescribe a sedating antihistamine (e.g., **hydroxyzine 25 mg PO TDS**) for sleep. No topical steroid yet.

- **Ward (24-72h):** Typically outpatient, but if widespread (>50% BSA), erosive oral (unable to eat), or hypertrophic, arrange follow-up.
  - **Mild cutaneous (localised):** Potent topical corticosteroid – **clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream** applied to lesions BD for 2–3 weeks. Reassess.
  - **Oral LP – symptomatic (pain, erosions):** Topical corticosteroids (e.g., **dexamethasone 0.1 mg/mL mouthwash** 5 mL swish and spit QID for 2 weeks) or **clobetasol gel** on lesions. For erosive refractory, consider **intralesional triamcinolone acetonide 10 mg/mL** into the perilesional mucosa (max 0.1 mL per site).
  - **Widespread cutaneous (or failed topical):** Oral **prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg/day** for 2–4 weeks, then taper over 4–6 weeks. Use as bridge to steroid-sparing agent.

- **Long-term:**
  - **Chronic cutaneous (recurrent flares):** Phototherapy – narrowband UVB (NB-UVB) 3×/week for 8–12 weeks. PUVA (psoralen + UVA) if UVB fails.
  - **Refractory/ hypertrophic/ nail/scalp:** Second-line agents:
    - **Methotrexate** 7.5–15 mg/week PO or SC (with folic acid 5 mg once weekly, no on methotrexate day).
    - **Acitretin** 10–25 mg/day PO (monitor LFTs, lipids; avoid in pregnancy).
    - **Mycophenolate mofetil** 1–2 g/day PO (for severe oral LP).
  - **Oral LP – long-term risk:** Regular monitoring (every 6–12 months) for malignant transformation. Avoid irritants (spicy food, alcohol, tobacco). Refer to oral medicine if dysplasia found.
  - **HCV-related LP:** Treat the hepatitis C (DAA therapy) – LP may improve or resolve.

- **What NOT to do (common pitfalls):**
  - Do not use systemic corticosteroids long-term without a taper; risk of steroid dependence.
  - Do not prescribe oral terbinafine or griseofulvin (ineffective).
  - Do not assume oral LP is benign: if erosive or plaque-like, biopsy to exclude SCC.
  - Do not give **isotretinoin** for LP (no role; can worsen dryness).
  - Do not use topical tacrolimus continuously on oral mucosa for >2 years (theoretical risk of lymphoma with prolonged use; reserve for refractory cases).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Lichen Planus | Psoriasis (Plaque) | Eczema (Chronic) | Oral Leukoplakia |
|---------|---------------|---------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Lesion color** | Violaceous, purple | Salmon-pink with silver scale | Ill-defined red, weepy | Whitish or gray, well-defined |
| **Surface** | Flat-topped, polygonal; Wickham striae (fine white lines) | Thick silvery scale (micaceous) | Excoriations, lichenification from scratching | Smooth, plaque-like; no striae |
| **Distribution** | Wrists, ankles, lower back, flexors; oral mucosa (bilateral, reticular) | Extensor surfaces (elbows, knees), scalp, umbilicus | Flexural (antecubital, popliteal); face in children | Usually unilateral, on buccal mucosa, gingiva, tongue |
| **Pruritus** | Severe (cutaneous); oral often asymptomatic | Mild to severe; can be itchy | Very severe (often worse at night) | Usually none |
| **Koebner phenomenon** | Yes (lesions at trauma sites) | Yes | Sometimes | No |
| **Response to scaping** | No scale to scrape; striae unaffected | Auspitz sign (bleeding points) when scale removed | No | No bleeding |
| **Oral involvement** | Very common (reticular, atrophic, erosive) | Rare (geographic tongue if associated) | Not typical | Only oral lesion, no skin disease |
| **Biopsy hallmark** | Band-like lymphocytic infiltrate, saw-tooth rete ridges, Civatte bodies | Parakeratosis, absent granular layer, elongated rete ridges, Munro microabscesses | Spongiosis, vesiculation, perivascular lymphocytosis | Hyperkeratosis, no interface inflammation; may show dysplasia |
| **Key differentiator** | Wickham striae + oral involvement + bilateral | Silver scale + Auspitz sign + extensor distribution | Flexural distribution + history of atopy | Unilateral, whitish plaque without skin lesions; must biopsy to rule out dysplasia |

- **Other mimics:** Lichenoid drug eruption (history of culprit drug; often more widespread, including trunk). Lupus erythematosus (photosensitivity, malar rash, positive ANA, granular IgG on DIF). Lichen sclerosus (white atrophic plaques, no Wickham striae; predilection for anogenital area).

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | What to Do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation** (common) | Dark macules at sites of resolved LP, especially in darker skin | Violaceous-to-brown patches, often persistent for months | Reassure; avoid trauma; use sunscreen; consider topical hydroquinone 2-4% for disfigurement |
| **Scarring alopecia (lichen planopilaris)** | Patchy hair loss on scalp, burning/itching, perifollicular erythema | Perifollicular erythema and scale; tufting; positive pull test | Early potent topical steroids (clobetasol lotion) or intralesional triamcinolone; refer to dermatology |
| **Nail scarring (pterygium)** | Nail thinning, longitudinal ridging, permanent split formation | Dorsal pterygium (scar tissue from nail fold over nail plate) | Potent topical steroids under occlusion; prevention best; deformity is permanent |
| **Oral squamous cell carcinoma** (erosive or plaque-type oral LP, 1–2% risk) | Persistent ulcer, pain, induration, cervical lymphadenopathy | Irregular, friable, non-healing ulcer at site of known oral LP; biopsy shows SCC | Urgent biopsy; refer to oral surgeon / ENT; CT for staging; surgery ± radiation |
| **Hypertrophic LP** | Thickened, verrucous plaques (shins, calves) that are highly pruritic | Painful fissures, risk of secondary infection | Potent topical steroids under occlusion; consider intralesional steroids; may need excision if refractory |

- **Late-presentation patterns (exam stems):** 
  - “A 60-year-old man with known oral LP presents now with a 2-month history of a non-healing tongue ulcer.” → Suspect SCC.
  - “A patient with lichen planus on the shins for years develops a thick, crusted, verrucous plaque.” → Hypertrophic LP vs. SCC.
  - “A woman with scalp itching and patchy hair loss 2 years after initial LP diagnosis.” → Scarring alopecia.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

- **Mnemonic: PRURITIC** (for features of LP)
  - **P** – Purple, Polygonal, Pruritic
  - **R** – Reticular (oral)
  - **U** – Underlying immune (T-cell)
  - **R** – Really itchy
  - **I** – Inflammatory (band-like)
  - **T** – Thickened granular layer (Wickham striae)
  - **I** – Interface dermatitis
  - **C** – Civatte bodies

- **Mnemonic: WICKHAM STRIES** (to remember associations)
  - **W** – Wrists, ankles
  - **I** – Intensely pruritic
  - **C** – Cutaneous + oral
  - **K** – Koebner phenomenon
  - **H** – HCV association
  - **A** – Autoimmune trigger
  - **M** – Medication-induced
  - **S** – Scarring (nail, scalp)
  - **T** – Treatment: topical steroids
  - **R** – Recurrent flares
  - **I** – Itch – treat aggressively
  - **E** – Erosive oral = risk
  - **S** – Squamous cell carcinoma monitoring

- **Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition):**
  1. **Always look inside the mouth** – even if the patient presents only with skin lesions. The oral exam is the quickest way to differentiate LP from other papulosquamous diseases.
  2. **Wickham striae are pathognomonic** – if you see them, you can diagnose LP without a biopsy (in typical cases).
  3. **Koebner phenomenon** – ask about trauma: new lesions after scratching, surgery, or tight clothing. This is much more common in LP than in psoriasis or eczema.
  4. **HCV testing is mandatory** – up to 1 in 4 patients with LP may have HCV, especially in certain populations. Treating HCV can resolve LP.
  5. **Nail LP can mimic fungal infection** – but LP causes multiple nails, usually with bilateral involvement, and no hyphae on culture. Look for dorsal pterygium (virtually pathognomonic for LP).
  6. **Oral LP >10 years duration** – risk of SCC increases over time. Biopsy any erosive or exophytic lesion.
  7. **Hypertrophic LP on shins is often resistant** – may require intralesional corticosteroids or even surgical excision.

- **Common mistakes to avoid:**
  - Prescribing oral antifungals for oral LP (white streaks are not thrush – thrush scrapes off).
  - Using topical corticosteroids in oral LP without monitoring for candidiasis (add antifungal mouthwash if long-term).
  - Ignoring a drug history (e.g., patient on hydrochlorothiazide or ACE inhibitor) – switch to alternative first.
  - Assuming oral reticular LP is always benign – even reticular forms can transform; periodic review is needed.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

- **When to call a senior (dermatology registrar/consultant):**
  - Suspected malignant transformation (non-healing ulcer, induration, cervical lymph node palpable).
  - Widespread erosive oral LP causing dehydration / inability to eat (consider hospital admission for IV fluids and topical steroid under supervision).
  - Severe hypertrophic LP failing topical therapy (consider intralesional steroids or surgical opinion).
  - Bullous LP with large areas of denuded skin (risk of secondary infection, need diagnosis confirmation).
  - Lichen planopilaris with rapid hair loss (need prompt aggressive therapy to prevent permanent scarring).

- **When to escalate to ICU / crash team:**
  - LP itself does not directly cause acute collapse. However:
    - Anaphylaxis to a drug (if suspected drug-induced LP and the drug is another category – rare).
    - Severe oral LP leading to airway compromise (extremely rare; if laryngeal involvement with stridor – call ENT/anaesthesia).
  - **New NEWS2 thresholds:** NEWS2 ≥5; lactate >2 mmol/L from dehydration due to oral lesions; GCS <15 (if sepsis from secondary infected skin lesions).

- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5 or rising:** consider admission for supportive care (e.g., IV fluids, analgesia).
  - **Lactate >2 mmol/L:** think sepsis (especially if hypertrophic LP with fissures or erosive oral lesions).
  - **GCS <15 (or any drop from baseline):** exclude hypoglycemia (from oral steroids if on high dose) or sepsis.
  - **Temperature >38.5°C:** source (skin infection, oral candidiasis superinfection) – seek senior advice.
  - **Inability to swallow own saliva** (oral LP) → urgent review; may need nasogastric tube or systemic steroids.

- **Summary escalation guide (quick reference):**
  - *Call senior:* malignant change, inability to eat, rapid hair loss, bullous disease.
  - *Call crash team:* anaphylaxis, airway compromise, severe sepsis unresponsive to initial resuscitation.
